_metadata.description: Wiggins & McTighe's Understanding by Design (UbD) framework — three stages, seven tenets, six facets, transfer goals, enduring understandings, essential questions, T-M-A learning-plan coding.
_metadata.owner: Chaz Clark (BYUI faculty-development; Architects of Learning week on assessments)
_metadata.runtime_strategy_rationale: Body MD ~14,800 chars / 4 ≈ 3,700 tokens — within the 'either' band per runtime_strategy_rules. Set to read_at_runtime because (a) this is one of ~12 sibling knowledge files an audit agent loads selectively per report focus, not a stable cache-friendly prefix on every call, and (b) consumers (canvas_course_expert, ira_program_alignment) cite back to McTighe/Wiggins source pages and benefit from first-class retrieval citation surfaces.

scope: The Understanding by Design framework — three stages of backward design, seven tenets, six facets of understanding, T-M-A learning-plan coding. Boundaries: does not cover assessment-type definitions (see assessments_knowledge) or CLO well-formedness (see outcomes_quality_knowledge).

facts.definition: A planning framework — not a philosophy of teaching, not an approach to teaching. Two key ideas: (1) focus on teaching and assessing for understanding and learning transfer; (2) design curriculum backward from those ends. UbD prepares teachers to think short-term and long-term about what they are trying to accomplish (Wiggins).
citations: McTighe & Wiggins (2012), Introduction; Wiggins video transcript 0:22–1:02

facts.definition: Learning is enhanced when teachers think purposefully about curricular planning. UbD helps this process without offering a rigid process or prescriptive recipe.
UbD focuses curriculum and teaching on the development and deepening of student understanding and transfer of learning — the ability to effectively use content knowledge and skill.

Understanding is revealed when students autonomously make sense of and transfer their learning through authentic performance. Six facets — explain, interpret, apply, shift perspective, empathize, self-assess — serve as indicators of understanding.

Effective curriculum is planned backward from long-term, desired results through a three-stage design process (Desired Results, Evidence, Learning Plan). This avoids treating the textbook as the curriculum rather than a resource, and activity-oriented teaching in which no clear priorities and purposes are apparent.

facts.term: Tenet 5 — Teachers as Coaches of Understanding
facts.definition: Teachers are coaches of understanding, not mere purveyors of content knowledge, skill, or activity. They focus on ensuring learning happens, not just teaching (and assuming that what was taught was learned). They always aim and check for successful meaning making and transfer by the learner.

facts.term: Tenet 6 — Regular Review Against Design Standards
facts.definition: Regularly reviewing units and curriculum against design standards enhances curricular quality and effectiveness, and provides engaging and professional discussions.

UbD reflects a continual improvement approach to student achievement and teacher craft. Results of designs — student performance — inform needed adjustments in curriculum and instruction so that student learning is maximized.

Key questions: What should students know, understand, and be able to do? What is the ultimate transfer we seek? What enduring understandings are desired? What essential questions will be explored in-depth? The designer considers goals, examines content standards, and reviews curriculum expectations. Because more content exists than can reasonably be addressed, teachers must make choices. Learning priorities are established by long-term performance goals — what students will be able to DO with what they have learned. The bottom-line goal of education is transfer.

Key questions: How will we know if students have achieved the desired results? What will we accept as evidence of student understanding and ability to transfer? How will we evaluate performance fairly and consistently? Backward design encourages teachers to think like assessors BEFORE designing units and lessons. Two broad types of assessment: performance tasks (apply learning to new authentic situations) and other evidence (quizzes, tests, observations, work samples). Check alignment: are we assessing everything from Stage 1, or only what is easy to test?

facts.term: Stage 3 — Plan Learning Experiences and Instruction
facts.definition: Key questions: How will we support learners as they come to understand important ideas? How will we prepare them to autonomously transfer? What enabling knowledge and skills will students need? What activities, sequence, and resources are best suited? Teachers plan lessons to address three goal types: Transfer, Meaning-making, Acquisition (T, M, A). Code learning-plan events with the letters to ensure all three are addressed. Teaching for understanding requires students to actively construct meaning (or misconceptions and forgetfulness ensue); teaching for transfer requires opportunities to apply learning to new situations with timely feedback. Teacher's role expands from sage-on-the-stage to facilitator of meaning-making and coach giving feedback.
All three stages must clearly align — not only to standards, but also to one another. Stage 1 content and understanding MUST be what is assessed in Stage 2 and taught in Stage 3.
When someone truly understands, they can explain concepts, principles, and processes by putting it in their own words, teaching it to others, justifying their answers, and showing their reasoning.
When someone truly understands, they can interpret by making sense of data, text, and experience through images, analogies, stories, and models.
When someone truly understands, they can apply by effectively using and adapting what they know in new and complex contexts.
When someone truly understands, they can demonstrate perspective by seeing the big picture and recognizing different points of view.
When someone truly understands, they can display empathy by perceiving sensitively and walking in someone else's shoes.
When someone truly understands, they have self-knowledge by showing meta-cognitive awareness, using productive habits of mind, and reflecting on the meaning of the learning and experience.

facts.term: Two Cautions When Using the Facets
facts.definition: (1) All six facets need not be used all of the time in assessment. In mathematics, application/interpretation/explanation are most natural; in social studies, empathy and perspective may be added. (2) Performance tasks based on one or more facets are NOT intended for use in daily lessons — they are culminating performances for a unit of study. Daily lessons develop the knowledge and skills needed for understanding performances, just as athletic practice prepares teams for the game.

In Stage 3, code events in the learning plan with T (Transfer), M (Meaning-making), or A (Acquisition) to ensure all three goal types are addressed in instruction. Too often, teaching focuses primarily on presenting information or modeling basic skills for Acquisition without extending lessons to help students make meaning or transfer.

A long-term performance goal stated in Stage 1 — what we want students, in the end, to be able to do with what they have learned. Examples: 'Apply mathematical knowledge, skill, and reasoning to solve real-world problems' (math); 'Apply lessons of the past to current and future events; critically appraise historical claims' (history); 'Create and perform an original work in a selected medium to express ideas or evoke mood and emotion' (arts).

A statement of what students should retain long after a unit — an insight that organizes their thinking about a domain. Example: 'Great literature explores universal themes of human existence and can reveal truths through fiction.'

An open, generative question used in Stage 1 to engage learners in 'meaning-making' that deepens understanding of the enduring ideas. Example pairs with the enduring understanding: 'How can stories from other places and times relate to our current lives?' (literature); 'How does where we live influence how we live?' (geography).

Factual knowledge and skills are NOT taught for their own sake, but as a means to larger ends. Acquisition of content is a means in the service of meaning making and transfer. Teaching should equip learners to use or transfer their learning — meaningful performance with content. This is the result we always want to keep in mind.

By 'assessing', Wiggins does not mean grading — he means judging how we are doing against the goal, coaching, giving information about how we are doing against the goal. Like a soccer coach who is judging without giving grades.

A school can value 'critical and creative thinking' in every program statement and a student can still earn straight A's at every school in America without it, as long as they are smart, compliant, do their work, and are thorough. If critical and creative thinking is the long-term goal, the weekly plan must focus on critical and creative use of content. Otherwise we will not get it. ETS pre-/post-assessment at a top-10 prep school showed no gain in critical thinking from 9th to 12th grade.
Three-bit flag indicating which of Stage 1 / Stage 2 / Stage 3 are evidenced in the audited artifact. Emit only when the audit examined evidence for the stage.
Classifier: aligned | partial | misaligned. Captures whether the three stages reinforce one another (Stage 1 ↔ Stage 2 ↔ Stage 3).
Classifier for Stage 3 learning-plan coding: acquisition_only | acquisition_meaning | t_m_a_balanced.
Subset of {explain, interpret, apply, perspective, empathy, self_knowledge} that any assessment in the course actually probes. Emit only facets the audit found evidence for.

sources: McTighe, J., & Wiggins, G. (2012). *Understanding by Design® Framework.* ASCD white paper. ©2012 ASCD. (pre_knowledge/assessments/Understanding By Design Framework.pdf; pages 1-6 ingested)
Wiggins, G. *Understanding by Design.* 10-minute ASCD video. Transcript in pre_knowledge/assessments/youtube_transcript.md (10 min).
Wiggins, G., & McTighe, J. (1998). *Understanding by Design.* (Original book, cited via McTighe & Wiggins 2012)
Chaz Clark. *Designing Assessments — Testing the Foundation* (BYUI faculty-development Architects of Learning week notes, 2026, pre_knowledge/assessments/notes.md)

/tmp/canvas_drafts/backwards_design_knowledge.json exists with shape=reference and non-empty facts[]
